HIGH COURT HIGH COURT OF AUSTRALIA.} PLAINTIFF, AND JOSEPH SARSFIELD SMIDMORE RESPONDE AND OTHERS DEFENDANTS, ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF NEW SOUTH WALES. Construction—Restriction on alienation—Gift for life with children or remoter issue—Power to trustees to pay income during life to u attempted alienation—Rule against perpetuites—Uncertainty—Modij words of will—Intention of testator. A testator by his will gave a share of his residuary real estate to trust for each of his sons absolutely.

By a codicil, after reciting that h desirous of making further provision for his wife, and of rendering i as far as possible the shares of his sons, and of making other provisio directed his trustees to pay the income only of each son's share to the he should attempt to alienate it, in which case they were to pay the ine a discretionary power, in case the son whose share became so fo married, to pay the income for the residue of the son's life to his wife, each son's death as follows: "That my said trustees or trustee seized and possessed of the said respective shares in my real and pe to which such sons respectively were entitled upon trust for such children of my said sons respectively as shall attain the age of years and such child or children of each of my said sons respectit under the age of twenty-one years as shall attain that age or die under age leaving issue and if more than one in equal shares and propo! between brothers and sisters, but so that the child or children co any deceased child of my said sons respectively shall take such as their parent would have taken if living." Held, that the power given to the trustees to pay the income to the the event of attempted alienation was good ; and that the words " OF AUSTRALIA. should be rejected as having been inserted by inadvertence, and that the remaining words there was a valid gift in remainder after each son's th to his children attaining the age of twenty-one years or dying under 'that age leaving issue, and that, in accordance with the principle laid down "by James LJ. in In re Bywater, 18 Ch. D. 17, at p.24, this, being a clear gift, 'was not cut down or controlled by the subsequent use of doubtful words parently implying that there had previously been a gift to great-grandchildren in remainder. a i Where a direction in a will appears to have been taken from « common form, with the omission of certain words, which, if they had been inserted, would have rendered the direction void under the rule against perpetuities, "the Court, in construing the will, will not read it as if those words had been inserted unless the direction is otherwise wholly unintelligible. Where a direction contains words which are apparently inconsistent with provisions elsewhere clearly expressed in the will, but are not sufficiently clear to control "them, the Court should either discard the words, or, if possible, modify them _ in such a way as to carry out the intention of the testator as revealed by the whole of the will.
Towns v. Wentworth, 11 Moo., P.C.C., 526, applied ; Lassence v. Tierney, | Mac, & G., 551; and Hancock v.

Simpson C.J. in Eq. : Smidmore v. Smidmore, 5 "SR. (N.S.W.), 492, varied, and affirmed as varied. his will and several codicils, of which the third, dated 23rd tober, 1860, is the only one material to this appeal. By the will the residue of the real estate was devised to trustees on trust to divide it into four equal shares, one of which was to each of his four sons absolutely.

By the third codicil testator, after reciting that he was desirous of rendering enable as far as possible the shares of three of his sons, that to direct that the income only of each son's share should paid to him during his life or until attempted alienation, in h event the trust in the son's favour should cease and the income be paid to the person or persons who would have n entitled to it, by virtue of trusts subsequently declared, in case H. C. of A. 1905. ——s SmipMorE », SmipMoreE. H.C. or A. 1905, ee5, SMipMorE v% SmrpMore.

HIGH COURT forfeiture be married, the trustees should have power to pa income from the son's share during his life to his wife, and and after the son's death to his issue upon certain remai which are particularly set out in the judgment, and upon the dispute in this litigation mainly turned. : The property in question was resumed by the Minist Public Works, and the compensation money paid into Court: the Public Works Act 1900. On 4th April, 1905, in a suit by the executors of Albert J f Smidmore, one of the sons, it was held by the Chief Judg Equity that upon the true construction of the will and @ the estates limited in remainder to the issue of the son fi either for uncertainty or as infringing the rule against perpetu and that the share in the residuary real estate devised son belonged absolutely to his estate and passed to his per representatives. § The appellant, in order to establish his claim to his shat the resumption moneys, took out an originating summons fo determination of the following questions :—Whether upor true construction of the will and codicil the appellant absolutely entitled to the real estate devised to him by the whether the estate in remainder in the real estate devised to appellant expectant on the appellant's death was effect disposed of by the testator, and whether there was a valid t in favour of the wife or issue of the appellant under the forfei clause in the third codicil. The learned Chief Judge by his ot declared that there was no valid trust in favour of the childr remoter issue of the appellant in respect of the real estate de to him by the will, that the power given to the trustees by third codicil to pay the annual income and produce of the real devised to the appellant to any wife of the appellant during remainder of his life in case of attempted alienation was ¥ and that therefore the appellant was not absolutely entitled the real estate devised to him by the will.

It was from the second of these declarations, relating to | discretion given to the trustees to pay the income to the W that the present appeal was brought. At the hearing of appeal it was agreed that the declaration that there was no Va OF AUSTRALIA. in favor of the children or remoter issue of the appellant treated as subject to a cross-appeal by the respondents. material portions of the will and codicil appear in the t. (with him Harvey), for the appellant. Assuming that over in remainder after the death of the appellant is bad, held by the Chief Judge in Equity in a previous suit for ction of the same direction, the appellant is entitled to simple.

First, there is an absolute gift by the will. Then, the purpose of preventing alienation, certain trusts in mainder are imposed upon the absolute gift. They having d, the remainder disappears and leaves the absolute gift, pject only to the trust in favour of the wife for life.
But that is repugnant to and inconsistent with the absolute gift given, and therefore fails: Lassence v. Tierney (1): vy. Watson (2). In re Wolstenholme; Marshall v.

Aizle(3) is indistinguishable from the present case. The appellant ot the remainder which the testator attempted to give the ndants, but the estate given by the will, which revives. The so far as it deals with the provision for the wife, must be | not as indicating a change of mind on the testator's part, s an attempt to do something inconsistent with what he has eady done earlier in the same document.

By the rules of truction the first statement of intention prevails: In re Dug(4); Jarman and Bythewood's Conveyancing, 5th ed., p. (Grirritn C.J. referred to Brooke vy. Pearson (5).] The provision cannot be divided so as to make it good in part bad in part, any more than in cases under the rule against rpetuities, If part of the limitation is bad the whole must go. remainder either goes back to the life estate, or there is an y as far as the codicil is concerned. RIFFITH C.J.—You must look at the whole will together, not ie part first and then at another.
Cannot the whole will be 1 Mae. & 551. (4) 38 Ch. D, 176. 2) A.C., 14. (5) 27 Beay., 181. 43 L.T., 752, H.C. or A. 1905. SMrpMoRE v SmipMore.

H.C. or A. yead as an attempt by the testator to divide the estate ii 1905. —— Smipmore v SmrpMore. HIGH COURT one to the son for life subject to certain conditions, and th after the son's death to his representatives ?] s Together they make up an absolute gift. Where an ab estate is cut down for certain purposes only, and those fail, then the absolute estate remains.

That is the The condition imposed is void for repugnancy. It is not of revocation, with a substituted disposition. The life est treated all through as existing, and the attempt to cut it by the limitation in remainder has failed.

The decision of the Chief Judge in Equity as to the remain was right. If the words of the limitation are left as they they are nonsensical. At any rate they do not suffi indicate which class the testator intended to take i event of attempted alienation, or at the death of the son. has evidently been an omission of certain words from a com form, "any child or children of" before the words "each of said sons," and, if they are inserted, the limitation infringes rule against perpetuities.

It is therefore void either for tainty as it stands, or as infringing the rule as to perpetuities missing words are inserted. [He referred to Seaman v. Wood Jarman and Bythewood's Conveyancing, 3rd ed., p. 549, an ed., p. 840.] There is therefore no class to take in the ev forfeiture, the restriction upon alienation is bad, and the orig gift remains. The words " but so that" &e., at the end of provision show that some words have been left out, because import a reference to great-grandchildren, and unless the ™ that were apparently omitted are read in, there is no mentio such descendants in the earlier part of the provision.

Langer Owen, for the respondents other than J. 8. Smidm The intention of the testator was to substitute for the absoli estate given in the will, a life estate liable to forfeiture in ¢ attempted alienation, with a provision for assisting the wifi children, and a valid remainder after the son's death. Itis an attempt to give an absolute estate and at the same impose conditions repugnant to that estate, as in In re D (1) 22 Beav., 591. | OF AUSTRALIA.

Re Wolstenholme (2), but a clear change of disposi'The result is a valid life estate to the son, subject to restriction upon alienation, with remainder to the son's lren or remoter issue. Even if the remainders over are bad, only go back to the son, and his personal representatives are led to them, but the provision for the wife is still good. The inal estate is cut down by the codicil to the extent to which codicil can operate.

There is a dependent relative revocation. The modification is to be adopted so far as it is good, and so far , it is bad the life estate is left surviving. [He referred to an v. Kynaston (3), and Hancock v.

Watson (4).} 'he limitations in remainder are good. First there is a gift to grandchildren whose parents attain the age of twentyand then to those whose parents do not attain the age of enty-one but who themselves attain that age or die under it age leaving issue. That may be a capricious provision it is not nonsensical.

The words said to have been omitted y have been purposely omitted to avoid offending against the le as to perpetuities. The Court should not hold the provision rtain if it has any intelligible meaning, although the class ated may be a strange one. As to the words at the end, so that," &e., which imply a reference to great-grandchildren, , having regard to the whole of the will, and the intention of testator as therein revealed, they are superfluous, the Court may strike them out: Towns v.

Wentworth (5). Striking out those words there is a class sufficiently clearly designated, at any the Court cannot say that there is such uncertainty as to the testator's intention. There would then be no conflict h the rule against perpetuities. Even if all the wordsare left , and the words said to be omitted are inserted, there is a class to e during the life of the son in case of forfeiture, and it does matter what may happen during the future.

The rule against nities in its extreme form, that a provision which by reason 'the rule is bad in part fails altogether, will not be extended 80 as to apply to cases which do not clearly come within it, eg., to (1) 38 Ch. D., 176. (4) A.C., 14, at p. 23. B SL (5) 11 Moo.
C. or A. SMIpMORE SmipMoreE. HIGH COURT prevent the distribution of income where there are pe existence who can take.

It is not a case of tying up the corp The Court will lean against applying the rule, and will not words which would result in bringing the provision wil but, taking the words as they stand, will endeavour to con them in such a way as to carry out the intention of the testa and, if necessary, will modify them for that purpose. The respondent, J. S.

Smidmore, appeared in person to subn Harvey in reply. The whole of the words, including the | n vision in question, should be looked at, in order to discove intention of the testator, and then the rule applied. The Cou should not, in order to escape from holding the provision bad, that, as the testator did not intend to give it to persons who not take, they must look for someone other than the pi apparently designated, in order to give it to them. [He refer to Dungannon vy.

Smith (1).] The words themseélves "but that" &e., point out clearly the persons who were i and show that in the earlier part words were left out which have designated great-grandchildren. Words similar to the w suggested were inserted in In re Wise; Frith v. Wilson (2). construction is more natural than that proposed by the respoi which would render the words "but so that" &c., wholly nugat The Court should have no & priori reluctance to adopt any p ticular construction, merely because it might conflict with | rule.
The first step is construction, the next the application the rule. The estate may not be divided in order to escape # application of the rule: Hancock v. Watson (3).

Nor should words of the testator, "as far as is possible," influence the in construing the provision. In any case, that expression 0 has reference to rendering the son's shares inalienable, Cur. adv. The judgment of the Court was delivered by ( ¢ & F., 546, at p. 599. (3) A.C., 14, at p. 22 T., 302. 3 CLR] OF AUSTRALIA.

GrirritH C.J. The testator made his will in the year 1856, and by it he directed his trustees to divide the residue of his real estate into four parts, and then disposed of those parts separately, giving one of them to each of his sons absolutely. The plaintiff, appelJant, is one of the sons.

By a codicil, the third, which he made in October, 1860, he recited that he was desirous of making further provision for his wife and of rendering inalienable, as far as possible, the shares of his sons except that of his son Thomas, and "of making other the provisions hereinafter contained." He then made a further bequest to his wife and directed that with regard » to the share of each son except his son Thomas, whether in real or personal estate, his trustees for the time being should stand _ seized or possessed thereof respectively upon trust to receive the income arising from the respective shares, and to pay the same to the sons respectively until they should do or suffer anything which, but for the provision then made, would have the effect of vesting the right to receive the annual income payable to them respectively, or any part of it, in any other person whomsoever, and that, upon any son doing or suffering any such thing, the trust in his favour should cease, and the annual income subject to such trust should be paid to or applied for the benefit of the person or persons who would have been entitled to it by virtue of the trusts subsequently declared in case such son had died. Then he declared that in the event of such a forfeiture occurring the trustees might pay the income during the residue of the life of the son whose share was forfeited to that son's wife or any future wife he might have, and, as to the destination of the share after the death of the son, he made this provision: "I direct and declare that my said trustees or trustee shall stand seized and possessed of the said respective shares in my real and personal estates to which such sons respectively were entitled upon trust for such child or children of my said sons respectively as shall attain the age of twenty-one years and such child or children of each of my said sons respectively dying under the age of twentyone years as shall attain that age or die under that age leaving issue and if more than one in equal shares and proportions as _ between brothers and sisters, but so that the child or children collectively of any deceased child of my said sons respectively H. C. or A. 1905. se SmipMORE v Smipmore.

HIGH COURT shall take such share only as their parent would have t living." The difficulty arises upon the provision which I have read. One view of the construction of that provision is' h the intention of the testator was to divide the estate amor such children of his sons as attained the age of twenty-one, and su children of any son's children dying under twenty-one as sh attain the age of twenty-one, or should die under that age leavir issue. If that is the true construction it is admitted to be infringement of the rule against perpetuities.

But in ordi arrive at that construction it is necessary to insert certain in the direction, so as to make it read thus: " Upon trust for child or children of my said sons respectively as shall attain th age of twenty-one years and such child or children of any chil or children of each of my said sons respectively dying under th age of twenty-one years as shall attain that age or die under tl age leaving issue," inserting the words " any child or children o There are, no doubt, cases in which words may be inserted i will, if necessary, to give effect to the intention of the But if these words are inserted the whole provision is rendered vo Another construction has been suggested. It is said that, if o approaches the construction of this will without any precon ideas as to what the testator meant to do, it is possible to read without infringing the rule against perpetuities. A third vie that it is impossible to say which construction is the correct 0 and that, as it stands, it is altogether uncertain what the t meant, and therefore we should hold the direction void on tha ground.

We are informed also that in a previous suit in whi the construction of this same will was involved, the Chief Jud in Equity came to the conclusion that either on the ground infringement of the rule against perpetuities or on the ground uncertainty the provision was void, without determining wh construction was the proper one, and the decision now under to us was based upon that conclusion. But, although no di was asked for by the appellant or the respondents on this poi appears upon the originating summons, and is admitted on b sides to be before us for determination. On the assumption that this direction was void it was conten — 80.LR.) OF AUSTRALIA. ~ for the appellant, that if it is void, then the absolute estate given by the original will remains.

As an authority for that we were referred to the case of Hancock v. Watson (1). There is no doubt about the rule; and, although it is clear that in one sense the testator intended to revoke the original gift ~ to his sons, yet it is equally clear that he did not intend to revoke it except so far as he could give effect to the new disposition contained in the codicil, that is to say, that he intended something analogous to what is called in Probate proceedings a dependent relative revocatiov.

In that view the result would be that there was substituted for the original absolute gift, a gift of the income for life until attempted alienation, in which case the income was to go over to the persons named, and a gift after the death of the son to his representative. It was contended for the appellant that such a provision in effect made a single estate, and _ that any condition against alienation of any part of it was void. _ But there is noauthority for that proposition. There is no reasor that we can see why a testator, if he pleases, should not divide the whole interest in his real estate into two parts, one for lite and another in remainder, nor why he should not give them 'successively to the same person. It is clear that in creating a life estate a testator may direct that the income be paid to the devisee for life until he attempts to alienate, with a gift over if that should happen.

Upon the construction which for this purpose we assume to be correct, the testator has attempted to give the estate in remainder to the same person. There is no reason of _ common sense why a testator should not make such a disposition if he likes, and there is no rule of law to the contrary with which We are acquainted. The only rule which approximates to it is the rule in Shelley's case (2).

But that is a technical rule only to be followed where certain technical words are used. There is, therefore, no reason why the testator should not have given this property to his son for life until attempted alienation, with a direction for the payment of the income during the son's life to other persons in case of such attempted alienation, and a gift in remainder to the son himself after his death. That is the conclusion to which the learned Judge came, and so far we agree with (1) A.C., 14. (2) 1 Rep., 930.

In that view the income would, in the event of an 1905. ee SmipMor« v SmrpMore. HIGH COURT to alienate, be payable to the son's wife during the son's life; to the diseretion of the trustees, and, subject to that, it wo to the persons to whom it was given under the direction have read. It is not disputed that, so far as that di relates to the life estate, it is not void as infringing the against perpetuities.

The decision of the learned Judge, t fore, on that point is correct. But we are called upon t whether the learned Judge was right in holding that th direction was void either as contravening the rule again petuities or as being unintelligible. We proceed to consider words of the provision from that point of view. ' The duty of the Court in construing a will is to ascertain possible, what the testator meant, without preconceived ides to his meaning, and to give effect as far as possible to his inten as declared in the will.

In the present case we know what desired to do, because he has told us.. He wished to disp his property amongst his sons in such a way as to prevent intestacy, and to render the gifts inalienable as far as pos It appears that it was present to his mind that there were lin beyond which the law would not allow that intention to | carried out. He therefore gives the real estate upon trust such child or children of his sons respectively as should at the age of twenty-one years. So far there was a vested estat the child or children of a son attaining twenty-one years. he goes on: "And such child or children of each of my said. respectively dying under the age of twenty-one years," indieati that it was his intention to deal with another class, that is tos the children of his sons who did not attain the age of twentyyears.

Then came the words "as shall attain that age or ( under that age leaving issue." That cannot stand of cour because if a child dies under twenty-one, he does not attain age. It isa well known rule that, where words in a will otherwise not sensible, or seem to be incongruous or irreconeilal with plain provisions, they may be rejected. The rejection o words " attain that age or" would be entirely in accordance wi that rule.

If we leave them out we get this: " For such child children of my said sons respectively as shall attain the a CLR. OF AUSTRALIA. nty-one years and such child or children of each of my said respectively dying under the age of twenty-one years as die under that age leaving issue." If there were no rule inst perpetuities, and no difficulty arising in the application of "the suggested rule, there would be little doubt, if that were all that was in the will. There could be little doubt that there was a gift to the children of the sons attaining the age of twenty-one years or dying under that age leaving issue, although, it is true, that might have been expressed in shorter words.

Bnt the fact that the testator made use of cumbrous and voluminous expres'sions to express his intention is no reason for declining to give effect to the words he has used. ' It is contended for the appellant that, if the language is to be modified at all, the words "any child or children of" should be 'inserted before the words "each of my said sons respectively." If » those words are inserted we have the result that the gift is void. Why then should they be inserted? If the whole of the context » shows that there must be some words inserted the Court may , insert them.

There have been instances in which Courts have + doneso. Butitis a consideration that must not be left out of mind, that the testator's intention was only to tie up the land as far as the law allowed him to do so, The will was evidently drawn up _ by a conveyancer, or by some one acting under the instructions _ of a conveyancer, and, if the words inserted are read in, it would _ have been merely a copy of a common form to be found in the _ books of precedents, the effect of which would have been to defeat the intention of the testator. And when we find a conveyancer has taken a common form, and has struck out of it the words which if left in would have rendered the provision bad, surely we are not justified in saying that they were struck out by accident rather than fora purpose.

If they were left out on purpose, then e Court would be doing a very strong thing in saying that hough the testator, having his attention called to the fact that retention of these words would make the disposition void, i kk them out, we should nevertheless reinsert them. That certainly ought not to be done unless tie Court is compelled to do 'it, and as the words are intelligible without the insertion of the words suggested, it seems to us that we ought to read them as H.C. or A. 1905, aed SmipMorE v. Smrpmore.
H.C. oF A. they stand. On that subject I will read a passage fi 1905. —, IDMORE Swipmore. HIGH COURT judgment in Towns v.

Wentworth (1): "When the main pu and intention of the testator are ascertained to the satisfaeti the Court, if particular expressions are found in the will w) inconsistent with such intention, though not sufficient to eo it, or which indicate an intention which the law will not p to take effect, such expressions cannot be discarded or modi and, on the other hand, if the will shows that the testator necessarily have intended an interest to be given which then no words in the will expressly to devise, the Court is to su the defect by implication, and thus to mould the language of testator, so as to carry into effect, as far as possible, the inten which it is of opinion that the testator has on the whole sufficiently declared." So far then, if there were nothing mor the will, we are of the opinion that it should be read as a gil the children of the sons attaining the age of twenty-one ye dying under that age leaving issue, and that the words "at that age or" should be rejected, as having crept in by a or inadvertence. But another difficulty is raised by the concluding words, " that the child or children collectively of any deceased child of said sons respectively shall take such share only as their par would have taken if living." Those words seem to assume tl wise take by an original gift, and to say that this should not so, but that the children of each son should take his parents sh in substitution for him. Now, there is a well-known rule of eo struction that a gift given in clear words cannot be cut dov a subsequent ambiguous one.

Whether the first gift is not is in each case a matter of opinion. If it is sufficiently cle enable the Court to give effect to it, then the rule applies, althou its application may be somewhat difficult under the words in later part of the will. The principle was clearly stated Lord Justice James in the case In re Bywater ; Bywater vy.

Cla (2) where he said : " My view is this, that this is not a case of inconsistent gifts, a gift of something in one clause and a difl gift of the same thing in another clause, in which case, no 1) 11 Moo. P.C.C., 526, at p. 543. (2) 18 Ch. D., 17, at p. 2 OF AUSTRALIA. rule is that the latter clause, if a Judge can find nothing the last expression of the testator's wish.

This does not to me to be a question of that kind. It is not a question ference between two gifts, but there is a gift and a direction that gift is to be paid, which direction is inconsistent with the of the gift itself. The question is whether that which is direction as to the mode of payment is not to be struck it as a superfluous inconsistency and an inaccuracy, because the ving effect to it would alter entirely the nature of the gift and e the gift itself totally different from that which it purports Of course that case and this are different, but the principle plicable to the present case.

A similar rule is stated in Jarman n Wills, 5th ed., p. 449: "It is to be observed, too, that a devise Jands, in clear and technical terms, will not be controlled expressions in a subsequent part of the will, inaccurately ing to the devise, in terms which, had they been used in devise itself, would have conferred a different estate, if discordancy appear to have sprung merely from a negligent it of adherence to the language of the preceding devise." 'Those "are not the exact words of any Judge, but they appear to us to ay down a sound rule of construction. So that if these words e construed by applying the rule that a clear gift is not it down by subsequent ambiguous words or by words used eurately or from want of attention, the gift to the children of dying under twenty-one years is not cut down by the passage h which we are now dealing. Another reason may be given why should not hold that the original gift is cut down by these words )as to produce an uncertainty or failure of the gift. It is possible mstrue these words as really giving a gift by substitution ) the issue of the children who die under twenty-one years, treating the word "only " as surplusage, and reading " so that child or children collectively of any deceased child of my said ons respectively shall take such share as their parent would have ornot may be a question for discussion if and when any child s under twenty-one years and leaves issue, and the question B.C. or A, 1905. aeet SuipMorE v.

HIGH COURT arises whether the child's share goes to his represent: to his issue. But the time at present is not ripe for that point. On the whole will we can see no suffici for saying that this provision is uncertain or unintelligil can we see any suflicient reason for interpolating the wo gested so as to make the provision invalid, or for refusing t effect to the plain primd facie meaning of the words in whie gift is contained.

The result is that in our opinion the testator has suecee c what he tried to do, 72, in giving an estate to his sons with a valid gift over in the event of attempted alienatio question which the learned Judge decided does not, view, really arise. The result, so far as relates to the of the money out of Court, which is the object of the proceedings, is that the appellant fails. But, holding the vi do of the construction of the will, the questions must be an ina different manner.

The first declaration in the order w there is no valid trust in favour of the children or remoter of the plaintiff in respect of the real estate devised to him. must be varied by declaring that there is a valid trust in fa of the children or remoter issue of the plaintiff. That, we is all that it is necessary for us to order. Harvey for the appellant asked that the deeree m reformed in accordance with the Court's decision. an estate for life determinable on attempted alienati remainder to his children or remoter issue.
We do not make declaration as to who are the members of the class of pe entitled. The costs of this appeal should come out of th of the appellant. Order varied uecordingly.
Solicitors, for the appellant, Makinson & Plunkett. Solicitors, for the respondents, other than J. 8. SI Makinson & Plunkett.
